Python automation: How would you build a: Use the Resource Manager, IAM, Storage, and Compute client libraries to enumerate projects and pull IAM bindings (flagging broad roles like Owner on non-human accounts), bucket IAM policies (flagging allUsers/allAuthenticatedUsers), firewall rules unused in recent flow logs, and resources missing required labels. Combine this with a billing export query for week-over-week cost spikes, score findings by severity, and output a report or feed them into a ticketing system rather than auto-remediating blindly.
Cloud security: How would you secure wor: Bind each Kubernetes ServiceAccount to a dedicated GCP service account via Workload Identity so pods get short-lived, automatically-rotated credentials instead of mounted JSON keys, and grant that GCP service account only the specific IAM roles the workload needs (not project-level Editor). Pull secrets at runtime from Secret Manager via the Workload Identity-bound identity rather than baking them into images or Kubernetes Secrets, and review bindings periodically to remove unused permissions.
Vertex AI and MLOps: A team wants model: Package the model behind a FastAPI service in a container with GPU driver compatibility, deploy on a GPU node pool with taints/tolerations so only ML workloads schedule there, and autoscale using a custom metric (queue depth or GPU utilization) rather than plain CPU since GPU-bound inference doesn't correlate well with CPU usage. Monitor latency, throughput, and GPU utilization, and roll out changes via canary with automatic rollback if error rate or latency regresses beyond a threshold.
Go optional: Where would Go be useful in: Go is the right choice for building Kubernetes controllers/operators, CLIs distributed to many engineers (single static binary, fast startup), and any tool needing high concurrency or low resource overhead as a long-running service. Python is better for scripts with heavy API/data manipulation and faster iteration where startup time doesn't matter; Bash is fine for short glue scripts and simple orchestration but should be rewritten in Python or Go once it grows past a few dozen lines of real logic.
Terraform state: A Terraform apply faile: First run `terraform plan` to see the actual drift between state and reality without applying anything, and use `terraform state list`/`show` to identify exactly which resources are inconsistent. Use `terraform import` to bring resources that were created but not recorded in state back under management, or `terraform state rm` for resources that need to be recreated, and always take a state backup before any manual state surgery so you can revert if the recovery makes things worse.
Cloud Run vs GKE: A company wants to sta: Cloud Run fits stateless, request-driven services with variable or bursty traffic that benefit from scale-to-zero and minimal operational overhead. GKE is the right choice for workloads needing fine-grained control - custom networking, sidecars, stateful sets, GPU scheduling, service mesh, or complex multi-container pods - and for teams that need Kubernetes-native tooling across many services. A simple heuristic: default new stateless services to Cloud Run, and only justify GKE when a specific requirement Cloud Run can't meet is identified.
Cloud SQL/AlloyDB: How would you trouble: Check whether the connection uses the Cloud SQL Auth Proxy/sidecar or direct Private Service Access, and verify Private Google Access and the VPC peering range are correctly configured if using private IP. Confirm firewall rules allow the GKE pod CIDR to reach the database port, check the Cloud SQL instance's authorized networks or Private Service Connect config, and verify the service account has the cloudsql.client IAM role - a very common cause is the pod's Workload Identity binding missing that role.
Pub/Sub reliability: A Pub/Sub consumer: Check the subscription's oldest unacked message age and undelivered message count in Cloud Monitoring to confirm backlog growth, then check whether the consumer is CPU/IO bound (needs more replicas/concurrency) or blocked on a slow downstream dependency. Review the ack deadline versus actual processing time (too short causes redundant redelivery), verify ordering keys aren't serializing throughput unnecessarily, and confirm a dead-letter topic is configured so poison messages don't block the subscription indefinitely.
Cloud Storage security: How would you de: Enable Public Access Prevention at the org policy level so buckets can never be made public even by mistake, use uniform bucket-level IAM (not legacy ACLs) scoped to specific service accounts/groups per bucket purpose, and apply retention policies/object lock for compliance-sensitive logs so they can't be deleted early. Use CMEK for sensitive data exports where you need key rotation control, and configure lifecycle rules to transition or delete objects automatically based on age to control cost.
GCP networking: How would you design GCP: Use a host project owning the Shared VPC with subnets per environment/region, and attach service projects per team so each team deploys resources into the shared network without owning networking config themselves. Centralize firewall rule management in the host project with tag-based rules rather than IP-based, set up Private Service Access for managed services (Cloud SQL, etc.), and use Cloud DNS private zones for consistent internal service discovery across all service projects.
GKE upgrades: A GKE cluster upgrade caus: Upgrade the control plane first (GKE manages this with minimal disruption for regional clusters), then roll node pools using surge upgrades (create new nodes before draining old ones) rather than in-place upgrades. Ensure every workload has a PodDisruptionBudget so the upgrade respects minimum availability during node drains, test the upgrade path in a staging cluster first, and schedule production upgrades during low-traffic windows with monitoring dashboards open to catch regressions early.
Helm: How would you structure Helm chart: Use one chart per application with a base values.yaml holding sane defaults, and environment-specific values files (values-dev.yaml, values-prod.yaml) that only override what genuinely differs, rather than duplicating the entire values file per environment. Store values files in version control alongside the chart, template only what needs to vary, and use a GitOps tool (ArgoCD) to reconcile the deployed state against the values file so manual `helm upgrade` runs can't silently drift from what's committed.
Network policies: A service should only: Start with a default-deny NetworkPolicy in the target namespace, then add an explicit allow policy with podSelector/namespaceSelector matching only the permitted source namespace and the ingress gateway's pod labels. Validate by attempting a connection from an unauthorized pod/namespace (should fail) and from the two permitted sources (should succeed), and confirm the CNI plugin in use actually enforces NetworkPolicies, since some default CNIs don't without additional configuration.
Service mesh: When would you introduce I: Introduce a service mesh once you need consistent mTLS across many services, fine-grained traffic splitting for canary/A-B testing, or unified observability without instrumenting every service individually - not as a default for every cluster, since it adds real operational complexity. Risks include sidecar resource overhead, debugging difficulty when mesh-level retries mask real failures or double up with application-level retries, and mTLS misconfiguration silently breaking traffic if strict mode is enabled before every service is enrolled.
Progressive delivery: How would you impl: Use Cloud Deploy or Argo Rollouts to shift a small percentage of traffic (e.g. 5-10%) to the new version, define success metrics (error rate, latency, business KPIs) that are automatically evaluated during the canary window via Cloud Monitoring or Prometheus, and configure automatic rollback if metrics breach thresholds rather than requiring a human to notice and act. Gradually increase traffic percentage on success, and keep the rollback path as fast as the promotion path so a bad release is reverted in minutes, not hours.
Release readiness: A production release: Add release gates beyond CI - a canary stage with automated metric validation, a load/smoke test against a staging environment that mirrors production traffic patterns, and a readiness checklist (rollback plan documented, on-call aware, feature-flagged if risky) before full rollout. Treat CI passing as necessary but not sufficient; the real signal that a release is safe comes from observing real or synthetic traffic against it before it reaches 100% of users.
Incident leadership: You are the inciden: As incident commander, focus on coordination rather than personally debugging - assign a technical lead to drive investigation, designate a scribe to keep a timeline, and own regular stakeholder updates on a fixed cadence even when there's no new information, since silence erodes trust during an incident. Prioritize mitigation (rollback, scale, traffic shift) over root-causing while impact continues, and once resolved, drive a blameless postmortem with concrete action items and owners, not just a narrative.
RCA: Walk me through how you would write: A strong RCA documents the timeline, customer impact, the technical root cause (not just the trigger - dig into why the system allowed that trigger to cause an outage), and explicitly separates the immediate fix from the systemic prevention work. Since this is a repeated outage, the RCA should call out why the previous fix didn't prevent recurrence, and action items should be specific, owned, and dated - converting the recurring pattern into a permanent guardrail (test, alert, or architectural change) rather than another one-off patch.
Error budgets: How would you explain err: Explain it as a shared budget for imperfection: if the SLO allows 99.9% availability, that 0.1% is a budget the team can spend on releases, experiments, and acceptable risk - once it's exhausted, the team should slow down and focus on reliability rather than shipping new features. Use it in release decisions concretely: if the error budget is healthy, ship faster and take more risk; if it's nearly exhausted, require extra review or freeze non-critical releases until reliability recovers, making the tradeoff data-driven instead of political.
ELK/OpenSearch: When would you use ELK o: Use ELK/OpenSearch when you need advanced full-text search, custom dashboards, or cross-cutting analysis that Cloud Logging's query language doesn't support well, or when logs need to live outside GCP for multi-cloud/compliance reasons. Manage cost with index lifecycle management - hot indices for recent, frequently-queried data on fast storage, warm/cold tiers for older data, and automated deletion or archival to cold storage past a retention window, plus filtering out low-value log volume before ingestion rather than indexing everything.
Admission controls: How would you enforc: Start policies in audit/dry-run mode so violations are visible without blocking deployments, give teams a grace period and clear error messages pointing to exactly what needs to change, and only move a policy to enforcing once most workloads already pass it. Keep the policy set focused on genuinely high-risk violations (privileged containers, missing resource limits, public LoadBalancer without review) rather than blocking on stylistic preferences, which is what erodes developer trust in the guardrails.
Security Command Center: How would you o: Route SCC findings automatically into a ticketing system tagged with the owning team (derived from resource labels), assign SLAs by severity (critical findings remediated in days, low severity in weeks), and track aging findings in a dashboard leadership reviews regularly. Suppress known false positives explicitly (with a documented reason and expiry) rather than letting teams ignore the noise, and periodically audit whether closed findings were actually fixed, not just marked resolved.
Secrets migration: How would you migrate: Enable Workload Identity on the cluster and node pools, create the IAM binding mapping each Kubernetes ServiceAccount to its GCP service account, and update application deployments to use that KSA instead of a mounted key volume - test in a non-production namespace first since the change is transparent to most client libraries. Once verified, disable and eventually delete the old JSON keys, and migrate any application secrets (not just GCP credentials) from environment variables or ConfigMaps into Secret Manager accessed via the same Workload Identity.
Landing zone: How would you design a GCP: Structure folders by environment or business unit (e.g. prod/non-prod at the top level, then team folders beneath), with one project per workload/environment combination rather than shared projects that mix concerns. Centralize networking via Shared VPC from a host project, enforce guardrails with org policies (restrict public IPs, require OS Login, restrict resource locations), aggregate logs and billing to dedicated projects, and grant IAM at the folder level where possible so new projects inherit consistent governance automatically.
FinOps: A monthly GCP bill increased by: Break down the billing export by project, service, and SKU to find exactly what grew, and compare against what changed in the migration (new resource types, higher replica counts, a different machine family, egress traffic patterns). Common culprits are unoptimized machine types, missing autoscaling limits, or unexpected cross-region/cross-zone egress; address the specific driver (rightsizing, committed use discounts, reducing egress) rather than broadly cutting resources, and validate reliability metrics didn't regress after each cost change.
DR/backup: How would you define RTO and: Define RTO (how long the business can tolerate the service being down) and RPO (how much data loss is acceptable) based on business impact, not technical convenience, then design backup frequency and failover architecture to meet those targets - a 5-minute RPO requires continuous replication, not nightly backups. Test regularly with real restore drills and full regional failover game days, not just backup-completed checks, since an untested backup is not a proven recovery capability.
Multi-region design: You have a multi-re: Use a global load balancer with health checks to route traffic to the nearest healthy region, and choose data stores with multi-region replication (Spanner, or a primary/replica setup with documented failover for Cloud SQL) matched to your consistency requirements. Automate failover detection and traffic shifting where possible rather than relying on manual DNS changes, and ensure monitoring and incident response playbooks explicitly cover the multi-region failure scenario, not just single-instance failures.
Platform maturity: How would you evaluat: Assess each dimension against concrete signals rather than opinion: reliability (SLOs defined and met, incident frequency trending down), security (baseline controls enforced by policy not convention, findings tracked to closure), cost (visibility and accountability per team, not just a total bill), developer experience (time from idea to production, self-service adoption rate), and automation (percentage of provisioning that's self-service versus ticket-driven). A mature platform has measurable answers to all five, not just anecdotes.
Gateway/API strategy: How would you choo: Use Ingress or the newer Gateway API for straightforward HTTP routing into the cluster; Gateway API is the forward-looking choice since it's more expressive and vendor-neutral for advanced routing. Add a service mesh only when you need mTLS and traffic control between services inside the cluster, not just at the edge. Reserve Apigee for external-facing API management needs - rate limiting, API keys, monetization, developer portals - that go beyond simple traffic routing.
MLOps lifecycle: How would you design ML: Track every training run's parameters, metrics, and artifacts in MLflow/Vertex Experiments, register a model version only after it passes evaluation gates, and require an explicit approval step (automated threshold check or human review) before promotion to production. Deploy behind a versioned endpoint so rollback is a config change, monitor prediction distributions for drift against the training baseline, and trigger a defined response (alert, retrain, rollback) rather than letting drift silently degrade accuracy.
GPU workloads: How would you schedule GP: Create a dedicated GPU node pool tainted so only GPU-requesting pods schedule there, and add matching tolerations plus resource requests (nvidia.com/gpu) on those workloads. Use quotas to prevent runaway GPU consumption by any one team, prefer spot/preemptible GPU nodes for fault-tolerant batch workloads to cut cost significantly, and monitor GPU utilization (not just pod scheduling) since idle-but-allocated GPUs are a common silent cost leak.
Cloud SQL backup restore: A Cloud SQL in: Confirm point-in-time recovery is enabled and identify the exact timestamp just before the deletion occurred, then restore to a new instance (never overwrite the original) so you can validate data integrity before committing to a cutover. Test the restored instance against a read-only query set to confirm the data is correct, plan the application cutover (connection string change, brief maintenance window) carefully, and communicate the incident timeline and data loss window transparently to affected stakeholders.
Regional failover drill: How would you p: Define the failure scenario precisely (full region outage), set clear success criteria (RTO/RPO targets, which alerts should fire), and run it in a controlled window with stakeholders aware, ideally starting in staging before production. Execute the failover procedure exactly as documented (not from memory), measure actual time-to-recovery against target, verify data consistency in the failed-over Cloud SQL/Pub/Sub state, and capture gaps in the runbook or tooling to fix before the next drill.
GKE private cluster: A private GKE clust: Verify Private Google Access is enabled on the subnet (required for private nodes to reach Google APIs without a public IP), and check that Cloud NAT is configured if nodes need general internet egress for non-Google endpoints. Confirm DNS resolves *.googleapis.com correctly (private.googleapis.com or restricted.googleapis.com depending on config), check firewall rules allow egress to the required IP ranges, and verify Artifact Registry permissions and Workload Identity bindings are correct for image pulls specifically.
GKE image pull failures: Pods are failin: Check `kubectl describe pod` for the exact error message first (permission denied vs not found vs network timeout tell very different stories), then verify the GKE node service account or Workload Identity-bound identity has the artifactregistry.reader role on the new registry. Confirm the image reference (region, project, repository, tag) was actually updated everywhere it's referenced, check network connectivity/firewall rules to the new registry endpoint, and verify any imagePullSecrets are still valid if using a non-GCP registry path.
GKE node pressure: Nodes show memory pre: Check whether pods are set with accurate resource requests/limits - if requests are too low, the scheduler overpacks nodes and real usage causes pressure; if limits are missing entirely, one runaway pod can starve others. Review QoS classes (Guaranteed pods are evicted last), check DaemonSet overhead consuming node capacity that isn't accounted for in scheduling decisions, and confirm cluster autoscaler is actually adding nodes in response to pressure rather than being capped by a low max node count.
Compute Engine migration: How would you: Containerize the application, validate it runs correctly in a container locally and in a staging cluster, and migrate incrementally (one service at a time behind a load balancer) rather than a big-bang cutover. Keep workloads on VMs when they have kernel-level dependencies, licensing tied to physical/VM hosts, require GPU passthrough configurations not well supported in your container platform, or are legitimately being decommissioned soon and not worth the migration investment.
Pub/Sub exactly-once: A team expects exa: Explain that Pub/Sub guarantees at-least-once delivery by default (exactly-once delivery is available as a feature but still requires idempotent processing for true correctness under retries), so the application must be designed to safely process the same message twice - typically via a deduplication key checked against a store before applying side effects. Use ordering keys only where sequence genuinely matters (it limits throughput per key), and configure a dead-letter topic with a bounded max delivery attempt count so a poison message doesn't loop forever.
Private Service Connect: How would you u: Use Private Service Connect to expose a service (yours or a managed GCP service) privately to consumer VPCs without VPC peering, publishing a service attachment on the producer side and creating an endpoint in the consumer VPC. Debug connectivity by checking the PSC endpoint status (accepted/pending/rejected), verifying firewall rules allow traffic to the PSC endpoint IP, confirming DNS resolves to the private endpoint rather than a public address, and checking the producer-side service attachment's connection acceptance list.
Terraform secrets: How would you prevent: Avoid passing raw secret values as Terraform variables where possible; instead reference secrets already stored in Secret Manager and grant the resource access to fetch them at runtime rather than embedding the value in Terraform config. Mark sensitive variables and outputs with `sensitive = true` so they're redacted from CLI output and plan summaries, store state in an encrypted remote backend with restricted access (state still contains secret values in plaintext), and ensure CI logs mask any secret-adjacent output.
Terraform import: A team created resourc: Write the Terraform resource configuration to match the existing resource's actual settings first, then use `terraform import` (or the newer import blocks) to bring it under state management, and immediately run `terraform plan` to confirm zero diff - any drift shown means your config doesn't yet match reality and needs adjustment before proceeding. Import resources incrementally, not all at once, and require a plan review before the first real apply against newly-imported resources to catch any unintended changes.
GitHub Actions security: How would you s: Use OIDC federation so GitHub Actions authenticates to GCP with short-lived tokens instead of long-lived service account keys stored as secrets, scope the associated service account to only the permissions that workflow needs, and use GitHub Environments with required reviewers for deployments to production. Pin action versions to a commit SHA (not a mutable tag) to prevent supply-chain tampering, and ensure secrets are scoped to the specific environment rather than available to every workflow in the repo.
Release rollback: A canary deployment pa: Treat business metric regression as seriously as technical metric regression - a canary that's technically healthy but drives down conversion or revenue is still a failed release. Roll back immediately if the business impact is significant and ongoing, since the cost of staying live and investigating usually exceeds the cost of a quick rollback; only continue investigating live if the metric drop is small, ambiguous, or plausibly caused by something unrelated to the release (seasonality, an unrelated incident) that you can verify quickly.
Toil reduction: How would you identify o: Track recurring manual work explicitly - ticket categories, time spent per task type, and repeated Slack requests - to find the highest-frequency, highest-effort toil rather than guessing. Prioritize automation candidates by (frequency x time saved) versus build effort, and treat toil reduction as a real backlog item with the same rigor as feature work, since it competes for the same engineering time and needs to be justified with expected ROI.
Chaos testing: How would you introduce c: Start in staging with well-understood, low-blast-radius experiments - killing a single pod, simulating a node drain - before ever running chaos experiments in production, and always have a clear abort mechanism. Test the failure modes most likely to actually occur first: pod crashes, node failures, and dependency timeouts, since these validate whether your health checks, PDBs, and retry logic actually work as designed rather than just looking correct on paper.
Logging cost: Cloud Logging cost is grow: Use log exclusion filters to drop high-volume, low-value logs (health check noise, verbose debug logs in production) before they're ingested and billed, and route logs you still want to keep long-term to cheaper storage (a Cloud Storage sink) rather than paying Cloud Logging's retention rate for everything. Keep sufficient structured context in the logs you do keep (request IDs, error details) so reducing volume doesn't reduce the ability to actually debug an incident when it matters.
Alert ownership: How would you ensure ev: Require every new alert to be tied to a specific team/on-call rotation, link to a runbook describing the response steps (not just the symptom), and justify its threshold against an SLO or known user-impact signal rather than an arbitrary number. Periodically audit alerts that fire frequently without action taken or that page the wrong team, and remove or fix alerts that don't meet this bar, since unowned or non-actionable alerts are what causes alert fatigue.
SCC operations: How would you operationa: Pipe SCC findings into your ticketing system automatically with severity-based routing and SLAs, prioritize based on both severity and exploitability/exposure (an internet-facing critical finding outranks an internal medium one), and track remediation to actual verified closure, not just ticket status. Provide a rolling report to leadership on open findings by age and severity so unresolved risk stays visible rather than accumulating silently.
SLSA/provenance: How would you explain S: Explain SLSA as a framework that gives you graduated confidence that an artifact was built the way you think it was - from basic build tracking to fully verified, tamper-resistant build provenance - and provenance as the metadata proving where and how an artifact was built (source commit, build system, inputs). Signed artifacts (via cosign/Sigstore) let a deployment target cryptographically verify an image wasn't tampered with after the CI pipeline built it, which is what Binary Authorization enforces before allowing deployment.
Golden path adoption: Teams avoid your g: Talk directly to the teams bypassing it rather than assuming - the golden path is usually avoided because it's missing a capability they need, too rigid for their use case, or has a worse developer experience than rolling their own. Fix the actual gap, involve early adopters in shaping the next iteration so it reflects real needs, and measure adoption as a product metric you actively improve rather than mandating compliance, which tends to produce quiet workarounds instead of genuine adoption.
Service catalog: How would you design se: Require every service to register ownership (team, on-call, Slack channel), a dependency list (what it calls and what calls it) ideally derived automatically from tracing data rather than hand-maintained, and a production-readiness status (checklist of reliability/security gates passed). Make the catalog the source of truth other tooling reads from (alerting routes, on-call paging, cost attribution) so it stays accurate through use rather than becoming stale documentation nobody updates.
Batch inference: How would you design ba: Use Cloud Run jobs or GKE Jobs/CronJobs for straightforward scheduled batch scoring with moderate scale, Vertex AI batch prediction for a fully managed experience tightly integrated with the Model Registry, and Cloud Composer/Airflow when the batch job is one step in a larger multi-stage pipeline with dependencies. The tradeoff is operational simplicity versus control - managed Vertex AI batch prediction requires the least maintenance but the least customization; GKE Jobs give full control at the cost of managing scaling and retries yourself.
LLM app operations: How would you operat: Monitor latency (time to first token and total generation time separately), cost per request/user, and treat prompt changes with the same rigor as code changes - versioned, tested, and rolled out gradually. Layer in safety checks (input/output filtering for harmful content, PII redaction) as a non-bypassable part of the request path, and instrument tracing that captures the full request - retrieved context, prompt, model version, response - so any bad output can be debugged after the fact.
FinOps showback: How would you implement: Enforce required labels (team, cost-center, environment) via org policy so cost can be attributed accurately, set budgets with alerting thresholds per project/team so overspend is caught early rather than at month-end, and publish a regular showback report so each team sees their own spend trend without necessarily charging it back financially at first. Move from showback to real chargeback once teams trust the attribution accuracy and have had time to adjust behavior.
Incident behavioral: Tell me about a tim: Structure this as a specific story: describe the disagreement (e.g. over whether to roll back versus continue debugging live), explain how you made the case for your position using data or risk reasoning rather than authority, and describe how the disagreement was resolved - ideally showing you prioritized user impact and incident resolution speed over being right, and that you followed up afterward to align on the decision framework for next time.
Leadership behavioral: How would you men: Pair on real incidents and change reviews rather than only lecturing - walk through your own debugging process out loud so the reasoning (not just the command) transfers, and give juniors low-risk but real production troubleshooting opportunities with you as a safety net. For Terraform safety specifically, enforce the habit of always reading the plan output before approving an apply, and use post-mistake moments as teaching opportunities rather than blame, since that's when the lesson lands hardest.
Design tradeoff: When would you intentio: Choose simplicity when the service's actual availability requirement doesn't justify multi-region complexity and cost - an internal tool with a few hours of acceptable downtime a year doesn't need active-active multi-region. Multi-region adds real operational cost (data consistency complexity, failover testing burden, higher baseline spend), so the decision should be driven by the actual business SLA and cost of downtime, not a default assumption that more resilience is always better.
Communication: Explain a complex GKE out: Lead with impact and status, not mechanism: what broke, who was affected, for how long, and what's the current state (fixed, mitigated, ongoing). Use a plain-language analogy for the technical cause only if it helps understanding (e.g. 'a routing update caused traffic to be sent to servers that weren't ready yet'), skip Kubernetes-specific terminology entirely, and close with what's being done to prevent recurrence and when a full writeup will be available.
Scenario - Terraform locked state: Terra: First confirm no other apply is genuinely running by checking the CI system and asking the team, since force-unlocking while a real apply is in flight is how you get real corruption. Once confirmed, use `terraform force-unlock <lock-id>` with the lock ID from the error message, then immediately run `terraform plan` before touching anything else to confirm state still matches reality and no partial resources were left in a half-applied state. Fix the root cause afterward - usually adding a CI timeout/cleanup step or a lock TTL - so a crashed job can't strand the lock again.
Scenario - GKE OOMKilled pods: Pods in a: Check `kubectl describe pod` for the OOMKilled reason and `kubectl top pod`/historical metrics in Cloud Monitoring to see actual memory usage trend versus the configured limit, and check whether the increase is a genuine per-request memory growth (e.g. a cache or connection pool growing unbounded) versus just more concurrent requests hitting a tight limit. If it's a leak or unbounded growth, profile the app (heap dump, memory profiler) and fix the code; if it's legitimate increased concurrency, right-size requests/limits based on real p99 usage and consider horizontal autoscaling instead of just inflating the per-pod limit, since that only delays the same failure at a higher traffic level.
Scenario - Docker image bloat in CI: A C: Use `docker history` or `dive` on the image to see which layer added the size, and diff the dependency lockfile change to find what pulled in extra weight (a new package with heavy transitive dependencies, or a build tool that shouldn't ship in the final image). Fix it with a multi-stage build so build-only dependencies stay in the builder stage and only runtime artifacts are copied to the final stage, pin to slim/distroless base images where possible, and add a CI check that fails the build if image size regresses beyond a threshold so this doesn't silently recur.
Scenario - GKE node pool stuck in NotRea: Check `kubectl describe node` for the NotReady condition reason (kubelet not posting status, network plugin not ready, or disk pressure), and check Cloud Logging for the node's serial console and kubelet logs to see if it's an image pull failure, a CNI/networking issue, or a quota/resource problem preventing the node from joining the cluster properly. If it's an isolated bad node, cordon and drain it and let the autoscaler replace it; if it's systemic across new nodes, check for a recent node image or network policy change and consider rolling back the node pool version while investigating, since letting the autoscaler keep adding broken nodes just wastes quota and delays recovery.
Scenario - Terraform drift after manual: Do not let the next apply silently revert the incident fix - first understand whether the manual change should become the new desired state or was only a temporary mitigation. If it should be permanent, update the Terraform configuration to match the manual change and run plan again to confirm zero diff before merging; if it was temporary, plan a controlled apply to revert it outside of peak hours with the on-call engineer's sign-off. Either way, treat this as a process gap - add a post-incident action to require any console change made during an incident to be reflected in Terraform within a defined window, so drift doesn't accumulate silently.
Scenario - Ingress cert expiry: Users re: Check `kubectl describe certificate` and `kubectl describe certificaterequest` for the failure reason - commonly a DNS-01/HTTP-01 challenge failure, an expired or misconfigured ClusterIssuer, or a rate limit hit on the ACME provider. Rotate or fix the immediate cert manually if needed to restore service, then add monitoring on certificate expiry (a Prometheus metric or alert on `cert-manager` conditions) well before the actual expiry date so a failed renewal is caught days in advance instead of being discovered by users seeing browser warnings.
Scenario - GCP quota outage: A batch job: Immediately check the Quotas page for the specific exhausted quota (CPUs, IP addresses, or a specific machine family) and either request an emergency quota increase through support or reschedule/shift the batch job to a region or zone with headroom if the workload allows it. Long term, set up quota utilization alerts well before the limit (e.g. at 80 percent) and either pre-request higher quotas for known growth or make the batch job quota-aware so it degrades gracefully (smaller batch size, queued retry) instead of hard failing at 2am with no one able to react quickly.
Scenario - noisy neighbor on shared GKE: Confirm the pattern with per-namespace resource metrics in Cloud Monitoring or `kubectl top pod --all-namespaces`, and check whether the offending workload has requests/limits set at all - unbounded requests are the most common cause of one team crowding out others on a shared node pool. Enforce ResourceQuotas and LimitRanges per namespace so no team can request more than their fair share, use PriorityClasses so critical workloads preempt lower-priority ones under pressure, and consider node pool separation or Kubernetes NetworkPolicies plus bandwidth limits if the contention is network rather than CPU, so a fix in one namespace doesn't require guessing at other teams' expense.
